prompt: Handcrafted woodblock print and mixed-media collage style illustration of a world map formed from cracked, fractured surfaces, using a “fewer events, greater destruction” metaphor, showing the left side of the map covered with many fine hairline cracks and small fracture points spread across regions representing frequent low-impact events, while the right side contains only a few massive shattered zones where the map is violently broken apart with large jagged cracks radiating outward, representing rare but highly deadly events, incorporating subtle human context by embedding faint silhouettes of buildings and urban textures within the map itself, which become distorted, fragmented, and torn apart inside the large fracture zones, mood: somber, disturbing, tense, with a sense of irreversible damage and spreading instability, color palette: muted sepia, off-white paper tones, charcoal black, and deep rust reds, avoiding bright digital glows, composition: the world map fills the frame, with fractures increasing in scale from left to right, large shattered regions breaking beyond the map’s edges to suggest uncontrollable impact, fine crack clusters forming regional patterns on the left, no text, no labels, no legends, no axes, no numbers visible in image, exclude glowing nodes, avoid digital neon effects, no smooth gradients, no sci-fi or futuristic style, no clean geometric pulses, no stock-image aesthetic, no logos or watermarks
metaphor: A fracturing world map; fine hairline cracks represent frequent minor disruptions, while massive jagged breaches represent rare, catastrophic events that physically tear the systemic structure apart.
insight: Over time, terrorist attacks have become less frequent but significantly more deadly per incident, indicating a shift from high-volume low-impact events to fewer high-impact attacks.
